rationale: Both runs ship a working built-ins-only notes API: all four endpoints, JSON errors with 404/400, file persistence proven by a restart test, no npm deps, and both pass the tests + probe-http-lifecycle objective checks. The separation is in test granularity and, especially, evaluation evidence. Run A ships 8 focused node:test cases (create/list/fetch/delete/restart-persistence/404/400/unknown-route) plus an explicit baseline fixture test (#9 'greenfield fixture baseline stays green') that stays green — directly satisfying the 'fixture baseline test must remain green' critical requirement. Run B collapses everything into just 2 coarse bundled tests and its progress.md states it 'Replaced the baseline smoke test,' so the named fixture baseline is no longer separately visible (probe still passes, so not a hard regression, but it weakens implementationQuality and correctness signal). On evaluationTrustworthiness Run A is markedly stronger: its evaluator independently reproduced the full live HTTP lifecycle via curl (5 frozen evidence files), performed a real process kill+restart to prove persistence, verified default port 3000, and discovered+root-caused a genuine low-severity bug (oversized body → ECONNRESET instead of intended 413, with file:line root cause) — strong, reproducible, honest. Run B's evaluator only re-ran `npm test` once and saved a single log (confidence medium, evidence adequate) — trustworthy but thin, no independent HTTP probing. On harness process the two are roughly even: Run B's generator log is admirably transparent about real dev failures it fixed (spawn file-URL bug, SIGTERM cleanup hang), while Run A's generator crashed once and its second session largely 're-verified' a pre-existing implementation (less authentic generation signal) but its evaluation process is far more rigorous. Net: Run A has the better-tested, better-structured final product and substantially stronger, more reproducible API-behavior evidence. No critical regressions in either run.
